comments.text: I think Erdos problem #3 is equivalent to $f(k)$ being finite for every $k$.

I'm not seeing this. Of course, if [3] is false for $k$, then $f(k) = \infty$. But how do we prove the converse? That is, how do we rule out the case where \begin{equation*} \sum_{n \in A} \frac{1}{n} < \infty \end{equation*} for all $k$-AP-free sets $A$, but \begin{equation*} \sup_{\text{$A$ is $k$-AP-free}} \left(\sum_{n \in A} \frac{1}{n}\right) = \infty? \end{equation*} My first thought was to try proving that if there are $k$-AP-free sets $A_1, A_2,...$, whose respective sums of reciprocals approach infinity, then they can be used to construct a $k$-AP-free set $A$ whose sum of reciprocals actually diverges. One method is to take the compactness approach: pick some positive integer sequence $n_1 < n_2 < \cdots$, and pick some (maximal) subset of $\{1,...,n_1\}$ that appears in infinitely many $A_i$ and add it to $A$, then restrict the sequence of $A_i$s to the ones whose initial segment is the same as $A$ and then repeat with $n_2$, and so on. It's easy to prove that $A$ has no $k$-APs, but the sum of reciprocals of elements of $A$ does not necessarily diverge (in fact, if the smallest element of $A_i$ approaches infinity as $i$ does, then $A$ is the empty set). So compactness without any considerations specific to APs won't be enough to solve this problem. Did you have a different approach (or perhaps a way of improving this one) in mind?

If the supremum is finite, then recursively one can construct finite $k$-AP-free sets $A_1,A_2,A_3,\dots$ with $\sum_{n \in A_j} \frac{1}{n} \geq 1$ (say) where the minimal element of each $A_j$ is greater than twice the maximal element of $A_1 \cup \dots \cup A_{j-1}$ (by taking $A_j$ to be a $k$-AP-free set with extremely large sum of reciprocals and then deleting all elements less than twice that maximal element). Then, the $A_j$ are disjoint, and the union $\bigcup_{j=1}^\infty A_j$ will have divergent sum of reciprocals while still being $k$-AP-free. EDIT: okay, merely keeping each $A_j$ greater than twice the maximal element of the $A_1 \cup \dots \cup A_{j-1}$ isn't going to be enough, due to $k$-term arithmetic progressions that have one element in $A_1 \cup \dots \cup A_{j-1}$ and the other $k-1$ in $A_j$. But if one randomly refines each $A_j$ down to a proportion $\varepsilon$ of the original set, and then further removes any surviving $k$-term APs of this form (which removes $O_k(|A_1 \cup \dots \cup A_{j-1}| \varepsilon^{k-1})$ of the original elements of $A_j$, where we weight each natural number $n$ by $1/n$ for the purposes of measuring size), I think one can still end up with a reduced set $A'_j$ with $\sum_{n \in A'_j} \frac{1}{n}$ as large as one pleases (by first taking $\varepsilon$ small, and then using the hypothesis of infinite supremum), with the property that the inclusion of $A'_j$ to $A'_1 \cup \dots \cup A'_{j-1}$ does not add any new $k$-APs, and the argument proceeds as before. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

This should be a reference for that (see the top of the second page): The Sum of the Reciprocals of a Set of Integers with no Arithmetic Progression of $k$ Terms, Joseph L. Gerver, Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society, Vol. 62, No. 2 (Feb., 1977), pp. 211-214 (4 pages). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The argument there has a slight typo (one should intersect $S_k(m)$ with $[2a_k(m-1),a_k(m)]$ rather than $[1,a_k(m)]$ when building $U_{k+1}$) but is basically correct. It's a little simpler than the repaired version of my argument because I try to keep $k$ fixed when doing the equivalence, whilst Gerver permits $k$ to slip to $k+1$ which allows one to avoid the probabilistic component of the argument.
One could assume problem [3] has a positive answer in order to workout a potential solution here.

Yeah, if #3 is false then $f(k)$ must be infinite for some $k$, hence for all larger $k$, and then one gets a positive answer to #169. So at least one of these two problems has a positive answer! That said, I'm not sure that the working hypothesis that #3 is true is actually of much use for this problem (it provides the weakest possible upper bound on $f(k)$, whereas this problem concerns quantitative lower bounds).
